overview.title: Aircraft Overview
overview.content:
# Citation S550 — Aircraft Overview

The **Cessna Citation S/II** (Model S550) is a twin-engine, light business jet manufactured by Cessna Aircraft Company between 1980 and 1990. It represents a significant evolution of the original Citation II, featuring uprated Pratt & Whitney Canada JT15D-4B engines, improved avionics, and aerodynamic refinements that elevated performance markedly over its predecessor.

## Key Specifications

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Manufacturer | Cessna Aircraft Company |
| Model Designation | S550 |
| Common Name | Citation S/II |
| Production Years | 1980–1990 |
| Engines | 2x Pratt & Whitney Canada JT15D-4B |
| Engine Thrust | 2,500 lbf each |
| Crew | 1–2 pilots |
| Passenger Capacity | Up to 8 |
| Cabin Length | Approx. 15.6 ft |
| Cabin Width | 4.8 ft |
| Cabin Height | 4.6 ft |

## Performance Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Max Cruise Speed | 403 KTAS |
| Long-Range Cruise | 350 KTAS |
| Service Ceiling | 43,000 ft |
| Max Range | Approx. 1,460 nm |
| Takeoff Distance (MTOW, SL) | Approx. 3,250 ft |
| Landing Distance (SL) | Approx. 2,700 ft |
| Max Takeoff Weight | 13,300 lbs |
| Max Landing Weight | 12,500 lbs |
| Basic Operating Weight | Approx. 8,700 lbs |
| Useful Load | Approx. 4,600 lbs |
| Fuel Capacity | 5,163 lbs (approx. 770 gal) |

## History & Background

Cessna introduced the S550 in 1980 as an upgrade to the popular Citation II (Model 550). The "S" designation signified substantial improvements — most notably the switch to JT15D-4B engines, which offered enhanced hot-and-high performance, improved fuel efficiency, and better single-engine climb capability.

The aircraft became a popular choice for corporate and charter operators throughout the 1980s due to its reliability, NBAA IFR reserve range, and the ability to access smaller regional airports. Approximately **160 aircraft** were produced before production ended in 1990, when Cessna transitioned to the Citation Bravo.

## Distinguishing Features vs. Citation II

- Uprated JT15D-4B engines (vs. JT15D-4 in the standard 550)
- Improved wing root fillets and nacelle fairings
- Enhanced bleed air system for better pressurization
- Updated instrument panel with improved avionics provisions
- Revised fuel system plumbing and improved APU provisions on some models

## Serial Number Ranges

S550 serial numbers generally fall in the range **S550-0001 through S550-0160**, though individual aircraft histories should be verified through the FAA Aircraft Registry and logbook review.

---

*This overview is a reference summary. Always consult the official Cessna Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM), Maintenance Manual, and current FAA records for authoritative specifications.*

airworthiness.title: Airworthiness Directives
airworthiness.content:
# Airworthiness Directives — Citation S550

> **Important:** This section provides reference information only. Operators must always verify AD compliance status through the FAA AD database at [rgl.faa.gov](https://rgl.faa.gov) and consult a qualified A&P/IA mechanic. ADs are legal requirements — non-compliance can ground an aircraft.

## How to Search ADs

1. Visit the **FAA Regulatory and Guidance Library**: [rgl.faa.gov](https://rgl.faa.gov)
2. Select **Airworthiness Directives** → search by aircraft model `S550` or `550`
3. Also search by engine model: `JT15D-4B`
4. Cross-reference with your aircraft's AD compliance log

## AD Compliance Tracking

For the S550, ADs typically fall into three categories:

### Airframe ADs (Cessna Model 550/S550)
- **Structural inspections** — wing carry-through, spar caps, fuselage frame areas
- **Flight control** — elevator, rudder, aileron hinge and attachment inspections
- **Landing gear** — actuator, downlock, and door mechanism repetitive inspections
- **Fuel system** — sump drain, fuel line chafing, and crossfeed valve inspections
- **Pressurization/environmental** — outflow valve and safety valve inspections

### Engine ADs (Pratt & Whitney Canada JT15D-4B)
- **Hot section** — turbine blade and vane inspections
- **Fan/compressor** — blade tip clearance, FOD damage limits
- **Engine mounts** — vibration isolation inspection
- **Fuel control** — fuel control unit replacement intervals
- **Oil system** — chip detector and magnetic plug inspection requirements

### Avionics & Electrical ADs
- ATC transponder, altimetry, and RVSM compliance (where applicable)
- ELT battery replacement requirements (TSO-C91a compliant)
- Wiring bundle inspections (specific SB/AD combinations)

## Notable Historical ADs (Reference)

| AD Number | Subject | Compliance |
|---|---|---|
| 84-26-03 | Wing spar inspection | Repetitive |
| 87-16-05 | Engine fuel control | One-time/recurring |
| 89-03-12 | Landing gear actuator | Recurring |
| 91-11-14 | Elevator hinge bracket | One-time |
| 93-07-08 | JT15D hot section | Recurring |
| 2006-06-07 | Wing fuel boost pump wiring — chafe/arcing risk inside fuel tank | One-time + recurring |

> **AD 2006-06-07 Detail**: Chafed electrical wiring harness in the wing fuel boost pump area could arc inside the fuel tank, with potential fuel vapor ignition resulting in explosion and loss of the aircraft. Required AFM revision to prohibit boost pump use for defueling under certain conditions, placard installation, inspection, and wiring modification.

> **Note:** The table above is illustrative only. Actual AD numbers and requirements must be verified through official FAA records.

## Recommended AD Tracking Tools

- **AviationAD.com** — digital AD tracking for owners
- **CAMP Systems** — professional maintenance tracking (subscription)
- **MyAeroCrew** — owner/operator maintenance management
- **Cessna/Textron Aviation** — factory maintenance support

---

*Always work with a certificated A&P mechanic and/or IA for AD compliance determinations. This reference does not constitute legal or maintenance advice.*

maintenance.title: Maintenance & Inspections
maintenance.content:
# Maintenance & Inspections — Citation S550

The S550 operates under an FAA-approved **Continuous Airworthiness Maintenance Program (CAMP)** or follows the Cessna **Inspection Time Limits** outlined in Chapter 5 of the Maintenance Manual. Most operators use a professional MRO or utilize the Cessna Service Centers network.

## Inspection Schedule Overview

### Phase Inspections

The S550 uses a **6-phase inspection program** (each phase approximately 300 hours, full cycle every 1,800 hours):

| Phase | Interval | Approximate Duration | Owner Community Cost Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 | 300 hours | 2–3 days | $20,000–$25,000 |
| Phase 2 | 600 hours | 2–3 days | $20,000–$25,000 |
| Phase 3 | 900 hours | 3–5 days | $25,000+ |
| Phase 4 | 1,200 hours | 2–3 days | $25,000+ |
| Phase 5 | 1,500 hours | 2–3 days | $75,000–$200,000 |
| Phase 6 | 1,800 hours | 5–7 days | Varies by findings |

> **Owner Note (COPA/forum data):** Phase I&II together typically run $20–25K. Phase III&IV also ~$25K. Phase V is the most expensive phase — commonly running $75K–$200K depending on aircraft condition and findings. Budget conservatively.

### Annual / 100-Hour Inspections

Part 91 operators typically perform an **annual inspection** in lieu of or in addition to the phase program. Charter operators (Part 135) must follow an approved continuous airworthiness program.

### Calendar-Based Inspections

- **6-month** — ELT, oxygen system, fire extinguisher check
- **12-month** — altimeter/static system test (IFR operations), pitot heat functional
- **24-month** — transponder inspection (FAR 91.411 / 91.413)
- **10-year** — emergency equipment pressure vessels (oxygen bottles)

## Engine Maintenance

### JT15D-4B TBO & Hot Section Intervals

| Item | Interval / Limit |
|---|---|
| TBO (full overhaul) | 3,500 hours |
| HSI (Hot Section Inspection) | 1,750 hours |
| On-wing life | Per P&WC service documentation |
| Trend monitoring | Required under most operator programs |

> **Cost Reality (COPA/forum data):** Engine overhaul on both JT15D-4B engines can **exceed $1,000,000 combined** at TBO. Hot sections alone run $80,000–$150,000 per engine. A **TBO Extension STC** is available as an alternative to full overhaul and can be obtained at less than half the cost of a full overhaul. Many experienced operators choose the extension STC rather than a full overhaul when engines are running well per trend data.

### Engine Trend Monitoring

Many operators use **engine trend monitoring** (ETM) systems to track:
- ITT (Inter-Turbine Temperature) trends
- N1/N2 RPM deviations
- Fuel flow anomalies
- Oil pressure and temperature

## Real-World Fuel Burn (Owner Data)

| Altitude | Fuel Burn |
|---|---|
| FL330 cruise | ~1,000 lb/hr total |
| FL350 cruise | ~850 lb/hr total |
| Takeoff/climb | ~1,100–1,200 lb/hr total |

> These figures are based on owner-reported data from COPA and aviation forums. Published specs show lower numbers — real-world burn at higher weights and lower altitudes is higher.

## Known Corrosion Hotspots

The S550 is a 35–45 year old aircraft. Based on owner community experience, the most problematic areas for corrosion include:

- **Under the lavatory** — moisture accumulation, often missed unless specifically inspected
- **Beneath the entry door** — drain area prone to standing water
- **Around belly antennas** — sealant failures allow water ingress
- **Wing leading edges (TKS aircraft)** — TKS fluid residue can trap moisture and accelerate corrosion

> Any pre-purchase inspection should specifically call out these areas. Ask your MRO to remove access panels in these zones.

## Known Recurring Issues (Owner Community)

- **Windshield cracking at attachment screws** — a recurring problem across the fleet; inspect carefully, replacement windshields are expensive
- **Fire extinguisher mis-wiring** — historically documented; verify correct wiring at each inspection
- **Control column bobweight support cracks** — Transport Canada issued Service Difficulty Alert AL 1999-05 on this; inspect at each phase
- **Landing gear actuator wear** — plan for replacement at higher times

## Landing Gear

- **Actuator overhaul**: Per manufacturer limits or AD requirements
- **Tire inspection**: Pre/post flight, replace per wear limits
- **Brake wear indicators**: Check during each phase inspection
- **Shimmy dampener**: Inspect and service per MM Chapter 32

## Avionics & Electrical

- **Battery capacity check**: Annual (minimum) — load test all aircraft batteries
- **IFR certification**: FAR 91.411/91.413 — static system, altimeter, transponder every 24 months
- **ELT**: Battery replacement per manufacturer (typically 1 year or 50% battery life used)

## Approved Maintenance Facilities

- **SkyWay MRO** — specializes in Citation S550; recommended by COPA community
- **Textron Aviation Service Centers** — factory-authorized network
- **Duncan Aviation** — Lincoln, NE / Battle Creek, MI
- **StandardAero** — multiple locations
- **West Star Aviation** — Grand Junction, CO / East Alton, IL

> **Owner Tip:** Always use an **independent MRO** (not a broker's recommended shop) for pre-purchase inspections. SkyWay MRO has specific S550 expertise and is frequently recommended by owners.

---

*All maintenance must be performed by FAA-certificated personnel. Reference the current Cessna S550 Maintenance Manual and applicable service documents.*

systems.title: Systems & Components
systems.content:
# Systems & Components — Citation S550

This section provides reference information on major aircraft systems. Always consult the official Airplane Flight Manual (AFM) and Maintenance Manual for authoritative procedures.

## Powerplant

### Pratt & Whitney Canada JT15D-4B

- **Type**: Twin-spool turbofan
- **Bypass Ratio**: Approximately 3.3:1
- **Thrust**: 2,500 lbf (11.1 kN) per engine
- **Max ITT (Takeoff)**: 855°C
- **Max ITT (Continuous)**: 830°C
- **N1 (Max)**: 101.5%
- **N2 (Max)**: 100%
- **Engine Start**: Pneumatic (bleed air or ground cart) or electric
- **Oil Specification**: Mobil Jet Oil II or equivalent MIL-PRF-23699

### FADEC / Fuel Control
- Hydromechanical fuel control unit (not FADEC)
- Manual fuel control backup capability
- Engine fuel shutoff valves — cockpit accessible

## Fuel System

- **Total Capacity**: Approximately 770 US gallons (5,163 lbs)
- **Tank Configuration**: Integral wing tanks + fuselage collector
- **Fuel Type**: Jet-A, Jet-A1, JP-8 (Jet-B with restrictions)
- **Crossfeed**: Available via crossfeed valve
- **Boost Pumps**: AC-powered, one per tank
- **Defueling**: Gravity drain or pressure defuel fitting
- **Fuel Monitoring**: Capacitance-type fuel quantity gauging

> **Safety Note (AD 2006-06-07):** Wing fuel boost pump wiring harnesses are known to chafe and present an arcing risk inside the fuel tank. Verify AD compliance before flight.

## Hydraulic System

- **System Pressure**: 1,500 PSI
- **Fluid**: MIL-PRF-5606 (red hydraulic fluid)
- **Primary Pump**: Engine-driven
- **Backup**: Electric hydraulic pump
- **Actuated Systems**: Landing gear, nose wheel steering, ground spoilers
- **Brakes**: Power brakes with anti-skid (if equipped)

## Pressurization & Environmental Control

- **Pressurization Source**: Engine bleed air
- **Max Differential**: 8.8 PSI
- **Cabin Altitude at FL430**: Approximately 8,000 ft
- **Air Conditioning**: Vapor cycle system (ground/low altitude)
- **Bleed Air Heat**: Available at all altitudes
- **Outflow Valve**: Automatic with manual override
- **Safety Valve**: Automatically limits differential

## Electrical System

- **Main Buses**: 28V DC (two independent buses)
- **Generators**: Engine-driven starter-generators
- **Battery**: Lead-acid main battery + optional second battery
- **External Power**: 28V DC ground power receptacle
- **AC Power**: Solid-state inverter(s) — typically 115V AC 400 Hz
- **Bus Tie**: Automatic and manual tie available

> **Owner Note:** The S550 uses engine-driven **starter-generators**, not alternators. A common confusion point — the aircraft has no alternator. Electrical failures during flight relate to generator or bus issues, not alternator faults.

## Flight Controls

- **Control Type**: Conventional — ailerons, elevators, rudder
- **Lateral**: Ailerons with roll spoilers
- **Longitudinal**: Elevator with electric trim
- **Directional**: Rudder with yaw damper
- **Flaps**: Electrically driven, 3 positions (0°, 15°, 40°)
- **Spoilers**: Ground spoilers — automatic on landing
- **Trim**: Electric pitch trim, manual roll/rudder trim
- **Bobweight**: Control column bobweight system — inspect supports per Service Difficulty Alert AL 1999-05

## Avionics (Typical Configurations)

Original equipment varied by year and customer specification. Common configurations include:

**Navigation:**
- Dual VOR/ILS/LOC receivers
- ADF (one or two)
- DME
- Weather radar (Bendix or Collins)
- LORAN-C (early models) or GPS (later additions)

**Communication:**
- Dual VHF comm transceivers
- HF optional
- Selcal optional

**Flight Instruments:**
- Dual AHRS or gyro-based attitude indicators
- ADC or individual pitot-static instruments
- HSI or RMI

**Autopilot:**
- Collins APS-85 or Bendix/King KFC-series common
- Flight director with coupled approach capability

> Many S550s have been upgraded with modern avionics including Garmin G600/G700 TXi, GTN 650/750, and ADS-B Out. Key STC holder for avionics upgrades: **JETTECH** (Centennial, CO) — covers Garmin G600/G700 TXi and GTN 650/750 installations. Avionics upgrades require STCs and 337 field approval or STC documentation.

## Oxygen System

- **Type**: High-pressure gaseous oxygen
- **Crew**: Continuous flow or demand masks
- **Passenger**: Drop-down continuous flow masks
- **Bottle Pressure**: 1,850 PSI (typical)
- **Duration**: Varies by configuration and passenger count

## Ice & Rain Protection

- **Wing Anti-Ice**: Bleed air — leading edge
- **Engine Anti-Ice**: Bleed air — engine inlet
- **Windshield Heat**: Electric
- **Pitot/Static Heat**: Electric
- **TAT Probe**: Heated
- **TKS (optional)**: Some aircraft equipped with TKS fluid-based ice protection on wing leading edges

---

*System descriptions are general references. Consult the S550 AFM, Systems Description, and Maintenance Manual for authoritative procedures and limitations.*

bulletins.title: Service Bulletins & STCs
bulletins.content:
# Service Bulletins & STCs — Citation S550

## Service Bulletins Overview

Cessna issues **Service Bulletins (SBs)** and **Service Letters (SLs)** to communicate recommended (and sometimes mandatory) modifications, inspections, and procedure changes for the S550. Unlike ADs, most service bulletins are **not legally mandatory** for Part 91 operators — however, they often reflect important safety improvements and may affect insurability, resale value, and charter authorization.

> Textron Aviation (successor to Cessna) maintains the service document library. Contact: **[txtav.com](https://txtav.com)** or Customer Support at 1-800-4CESSNA.

## Service Bulletin Categories

| Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Alert SB** | Urgent — address known safety concern. Strongly recommended. |
| **Mandatory SB** | Required by specific AD or charter authority. |
| **Recommended SB** | Non-mandatory improvements. |
| **Optional SB** | Enhancements, modifications, upgrades. |

## Common SB Areas for S550

### Structural
- Wing spar and attachment inspection procedures
- Fuselage skin lap joint inspections
- Carry-through structure refinements

### Powerplant
- Engine mount bracket inspection/replacement
- JT15D-4B bulletin compliance (issued by P&WC)
- Nacelle acoustic improvements

### Avionics & Electrical
- Wiring bundle chafe protection improvements (see also AD 2006-06-07 on fuel boost pump wiring)
- Transponder and encoding altimeter updates
- Lighting system upgrades (incandescent to LED)
- **Fire extinguisher mis-wiring correction** — historically documented issue where fire extinguishing bottles were wired to wrong terminal studs; verify correct wiring at each inspection

### Systems
- Anti-skid system improvements (where equipped)
- Fuel boost pump relay updates
- Pressurization outflow valve improvements
- **Control column bobweight support inspection** — Transport Canada Service Difficulty Alert AL 1999-05 (cracks found in upper flange area; can interfere with elevator control)

## Supplemental Type Certificates (STCs)

STCs allow owners to install equipment or make modifications beyond the original type certificate. Common S550 STCs include:

### Avionics Upgrades
| Modification | STC Holder | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Garmin G600/G700 TXi retrofit | JETTECH (Centennial, CO) | Glass cockpit primary flight display |
| GTN 750/650 GPS/NAV/COMM | JETTECH / Garmin | WAAS LPV approaches |
| ADS-B Out (DO-260B) | Various | FAA mandate compliant |
| TCAS II / ACAS II | Various | Traffic awareness |
| XM Weather / SiriusXM | Various | Cockpit weather datalink |
| Synthetic Vision | Garmin, others | Situational awareness |

> **JETTECH** in Centennial, CO is the primary STC holder for Garmin glass panel upgrades (G600/G700 TXi, GTN 650/750) on the S550. Well-known in the COPA community.

### Performance & Systems
| Modification | STC Holder | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Winglet installation | Various (verify) | Fuel efficiency improvement |
| Vortex generators | Micro AeroDynamics | Stall speed/handling |
| LED landing/taxi lights | Whelen, Precise Flight | Energy efficiency |
| Oxygen system upgrade | Various | Extended endurance |
| Enhanced Ground Prox (EGPWS) | Honeywell | CFIT protection |
| TBO Extension STC | Various | Alternative to full engine overhaul at TBO |

### Interior & Cabin
- Seat belt/harness upgrade STCs
- Cabin entertainment system installations
- Emergency exit light upgrades

## How to Verify STC Applicability

1. **FAA DRS (Dynamic Regulatory System)**: [drs.faa.gov](https://drs.faa.gov) — search by aircraft make/model
2. **STC Holder directly** — contact the STC holder for applicability to specific serial numbers
3. **Your A&P/IA** — verify the STC data package covers your specific aircraft configuration

## Service Document Acquisition

- **Textron Aviation Customer Support**: Primary source for Cessna SBs
- **CessnaOwners.org**: Cessna Owners Association member resource
- **COPA (Citation Owners and Pilots Association)**: [copa.org](https://copa.org) — Citation-specific resources
- **Aircraft Records**: Any aircraft purchase should include full SB compliance records

---

*Service bulletin compliance is a business and safety decision. Consult with your aircraft maintenance provider and insurance carrier when evaluating SB compliance.*

economics.title: Operating Economics
economics.content:
# Operating Economics — Citation S550

The Citation S550 has historically offered competitive operating economics for a light business jet, particularly for operators flying 200–400 hours annually. This section provides general reference data — actual costs vary significantly by aircraft condition, location, utilization, and operator.

## Acquisition Cost (Market Reference)

| Condition | Approximate Price Range (USD) |
|---|---|
| Low-time, well-maintained | $1,200,000 – $2,000,000 |
| Average condition | $700,000 – $1,200,000 |
| Project aircraft / needs work | $300,000 – $700,000 |

> **2025 PAPI Market Data:** Average market value approximately **$840,000** (2025 PAPI/Aircraft Bluebook). Values vary significantly with avionics package, engine time, and compliance status. ADS-B Out compliance is a hard floor for marketable aircraft.

## Insurance Costs (Owner Community Data)

| Pilot Qualification Level | Annual Premium |
|---|---|
| Qualified pilots (3,000 total hrs, 1,000 MEL, 50 in type) | $9,800 – $12,600/yr |
| Less experienced / lower time | $26,000 – $42,000/yr |

> **Underwriter Requirements (typical):** Hull insurers commonly require 3,000 total hours, 1,000 multi-engine land hours, and 50 hours in type. Pilots who don't meet these minimums pay dramatically higher premiums. Some insurers will not write the risk at any price without minimum qualifications.

## Annual Fixed Costs (Estimated)

| Item | Annual Cost (USD) |
|---|---|
| Insurance (qualified pilot, ~200 hrs/year) | $10,000 – $13,000 |
| Hangar (medium-sized metro area) | $12,000 – $36,000 |
| Pilot (if employed — full time) | $85,000 – $160,000 |
| Scheduled maintenance reserve | $80,000 – $120,000 |
| Avionics / subscriptions | $3,000 – $8,000 |
| Crew training (annual) | $8,000 – $20,000 |
| **Total Fixed (estimated)** | **$200,000 – $360,000/yr** |

## Variable Costs Per Hour (Estimated)

| Item | Cost Per Flight Hour (USD) |
|---|---|
| Jet-A fuel (165–193 gal/hr × ~$6/gal) | $990 – $1,160 |
| Engine reserves (both engines) | $200 – $350 |
| Airframe reserves | $100 – $200 |
| Landing/handling fees | $50 – $150 |
| Navigation/overfly fees | $0 – $50 |
| **Total Variable (estimated)** | **$1,340 – $1,910/hr** |

> **Real-world fuel burn data (COPA/forum):** At FL330, owners report ~1,000 lb/hr total; at FL350 approximately 850 lb/hr. Published hourly fuel burn is ~165–193 GPH (roughly 1,100–1,300 lbs/hr at sea level; lower at altitude). Budget ~$1,000/hr fuel at current Jet-A prices.

## Engine Reserve Planning

| Event | Cost Estimate |
|---|---|
| Hot Section Inspection (HSI) per engine (at 1,750 hrs) | $80,000 – $150,000 |
| Full overhaul per engine (at TBO 3,500 hrs) | $400,000 – $600,000+ |
| Both engines at TBO | $1,000,000+ |
| TBO Extension STC (both engines) | Approximately 40–50% of full overhaul cost |

> Engine costs are the single largest financial risk in S550 ownership. Track hours carefully and budget accordingly. Many experienced operators opt for the TBO Extension STC over full overhaul when engines are running well per trend monitoring.

## Phase Inspection Cost Planning

| Inspection | Typical Cost |
|---|---|
| Phase I & II | $20,000 – $25,000 each |
| Phase III & IV | ~$25,000 each |
| Phase V | $75,000 – $200,000 |

## Charter Revenue Potential

Operators placing the aircraft on a Part 135 certificate can offset costs:

| Route Type | Typical Charter Rate (USD) |
|---|---|
| Short hop (1–2 hrs) | $4,000 – $8,000 |
| Medium trip (2–4 hrs) | $8,000 – $18,000 |
| Transcontinental | $20,000 – $35,000 |

> Charter placement requires FAA Part 135 certification, additional insurance, and charter management fee (typically 15–25% of revenue).

## Cost Comparison vs. Alternatives

| Aircraft | Hourly Op Cost | Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| Citation S550 | $1,300–$1,900 | $700K–$2M |
| Citation II (550) | $1,200–$1,800 | $500K–$1.5M |
| Citation Bravo (550B) | $1,400–$2,000 | $1.5M–$3.5M |
| Learjet 35/36 | $1,500–$2,200 | $1M–$3M |
| Hawker 400XP | $1,600–$2,300 | $2M–$4.5M |

## Depreciation & Residual Value

The S550 is a mature aircraft with relatively stable values in the sub-$2M range. Key value drivers:

- **Engine hours/cycles** — time remaining to hot section or overhaul
- **Avionics package** — glass cockpit significantly improves marketability
- **ADS-B Out compliance** — mandatory; non-equipped aircraft are heavily discounted
- **AD compliance** — complete and documented compliance is essential
- **Paint and interior** — cosmetic condition affects perception and value

---

*All cost figures are estimates for planning purposes only. Engage an aviation financial advisor, aircraft management company, or tax professional for specific guidance.*

training.title: Training & Resources
training.content:
# Training & Resources — Citation S550

Proper type-specific training is essential for safe Citation S550 operations. The aircraft is type-certificated for single-pilot operations with appropriate training, though many operators fly with two pilots for safety and insurance requirements.

## Type Rating Requirement

The S550 requires a **CE-500 type rating** for pilot-in-command privileges.

- **Regulatory Basis**: 14 CFR Part 61.31(a)(2) — aircraft certificated for jet propulsion
- **Initial Training**: Ground school + simulator/aircraft training + FAA checkride
- **Recurrent Training**: Annual recurrent required to maintain currency
- **Single-Pilot Authorization**: Requires separate single-pilot authorization (see below)

## Single-Pilot Operations

The CE-500/S550 was certified under **FAR Part 25 transport category** requiring minimum two crew. Single-pilot operations are permitted only via a **FAA Single-Pilot Exemption** (not a standard "S" designation):

- **Minimum experience for exemption eligibility**: 500 turbine PIC or SIC hours
- **Training required**: Approved single-pilot training program (FlightSafety or equivalent)
- **Checkride**: Separate single-pilot practical test required
- **Validity**: 12 calendar months — annual recurrent required to maintain
- **Geographic limitation**: **Part 91 US airspace only** — exemption is NOT valid internationally without separate authorization from foreign CAA
- **Part 135**: Single-pilot exemption does NOT apply to commercial Part 135 operations

> **Critical Note:** Multiple pilots have been grounded internationally and aircraft impounded when attempting to use the US single-pilot exemption in foreign countries. Always verify authorization before international single-pilot operations.

## Approved Training Organizations

### FlightSafety International
- **Programs**: CE-500/S550 initial and recurrent (factory-authorized provider)
- **Location**: Atlanta, GA (pilot training); Wichita, KS (maintenance)
- **Simulators**: Level D FFS (Citation family)
- **Website**: [flightsafety.com](https://flightsafety.com)
- **Cost**: Initial ~$20,000–$25,000 | Recurrent ~$8,000–$15,000
- **Notes**: Single Pilot Exemption training available

### CAE / SIMCOM (formerly SimuFlite)
- **Programs**: CE-500 series training
- **Simulators**: Full Flight Simulator
- **Locations**: Dallas, TX; Orlando, FL; Scottsdale, AZ; others
- **Historical SimuFlite pricing**: Citation II initial ~$15,200 (recurrent ~4 days)
- **Website**: [cae.com](https://cae.com) / [simcom.com](https://simcom.com)

### Citation Factory Training (Textron Aviation)
- **Location**: Wichita, KS
- **Contact**: Textron Aviation Training Division
- **Notes**: Factory-direct training programs for all Citation models

## Training Curriculum Overview

### Ground School (Typical — 3–5 days)
- Aircraft systems (powerplant, electrical, hydraulic, pressurization)
- Normal, abnormal, and emergency procedures
- Limitations and performance
- Weight and balance
- Meteorology and high-altitude operations
- RVSM (if applicable)
- CRM / SRM (if single-pilot)
- Single-Pilot Resource Management (SRM) — additional emphasis for SP-exemption candidates

### Simulator Training (Typical — 8–16 hours)
- Normal procedures: startup, taxi, takeoff, climb, cruise, descent, landing
- Instrument approaches (ILS, VOR, GPS/RNAV, circling)
- Abnormal procedures: single-engine, hydraulic failure, pressurization loss
- Emergency procedures: engine fire, smoke/fumes, emergency descent
- Rejected takeoff and V1 cut
- Windshear and unusual attitudes

## Currency Requirements (FAR Part 91)

| Requirement | Interval |
|---|---|
| Flight review (BFR) | 24 months |
| Instrument currency (approaches + holds + intercepting) | 6 months |
| Passenger carrying currency (3 T/O and landings) | 90 days |
| Single-pilot authorization | Annual |
| PIC recurrency (strongly recommended) | Annual |

## Study Resources

### Official Publications
- **Airplane Flight Manual (AFM)** — required on board, primary reference
- **Pilot Operating Handbook (POH)** — operations guidance
- **Quick Reference Handbook (QRH)** — abnormal/emergency checklists
- **Weight & Balance Manual** — current for your specific serial number

### Industry Resources
- **COPA (Citation Owners and Pilots Association)**: [copa.org](https://copa.org) — forums, safety data, member resources
- **CessnaOwners.org**: Owner association with technical resources
- **NBAA** — safety resources, charter regulations, international ops guidance
- **FAA Safety Team (FAASTeam)**: [faasafety.gov](https://faasafety.gov)
- **CST Flight Services**: [cstflightservices.com](https://cstflightservices.com) — single-pilot exemption guidance and training

### Online Communities
- **COPA Forums** (copa.org): Members-only, Citation-focused community — best resource for operational knowledge
- **Beechtalk / JetAviators**: Pilot forums with Citation-specific threads
- **Professional Pilots Rumour Network (PPRuNe)**: [pprune.org](https://pprune.org)
- **Aviation News Talk Podcast**: Episode 385 covers the N666DS San Diego accident in detail

---

*Training requirements may vary based on pilot certificate, ratings held, and specific operating authorization. Always verify with your training provider and the FAA.*

community.title: Community & Forums
community.content:
# Community & Forums — Citation S550

The Citation community is active and supportive. Owners, operators, and pilots share technical knowledge, maintenance experiences, and operational insights through several key organizations and online platforms.

## Primary Organizations

### COPA — Citation Owners and Pilots Association
- **Website**: [copa.org](https://copa.org)
- **Description**: The definitive Citation community organization. Members-only forums with decades of institutional knowledge, type-specific safety data, and direct access to Citation experts.
- **Benefits**: Forums, safety analysis, annual convention, technical webinars
- **Membership**: Annual fee — worth every dollar for Citation owners
- **Recommended**: First resource for any Citation owner/operator

### NBAA — National Business Aviation Association
- **Website**: [nbaa.org](https://nbaa.org)
- **Description**: Industry association for business aviation. Resources on Part 135, international operations, safety, and advocacy.
- **Annual Convention**: NBAA-BACE — largest business aviation trade show

### Cessna Owners Organization
- **Website**: [cessnaowners.org](https://cessnaowners.org)
- **Description**: Broader Cessna community — useful for cross-referencing with other Citation variants and piston Cessna knowledge base.

## Online Forums & Communities

### COPA Forums (copa.org)
- **Access**: Members only
- **Best for**: Technical questions, maintenance experiences, operational advice, pre-purchase guidance
- **Sections**: Model-specific subforums including S550/Citation II family

### Beechtalk
- Active general aviation forum with Citation/turbine-specific threads
- Free to join; good source for real-world owner opinions

### AirplaneForum.com
- General aviation forum with business jet section

### Reddit — r/aviationmaintenance, r/flying, r/bizjets
- General aviation communities — less Citation-specific but broad reach

### Professional Pilots of America (ProPilot)
- Professional pilot community forum

## Facebook Groups
- **Citation Jet Owners & Pilots** — active group for Citation community
- **Citation II/S550 Owners** — search for model-specific groups

## Aviation Podcasts & Video

- **Blancolirio (Juan Browne)** — YouTube channel covering aviation accidents and safety; frequently covers business jet incidents including Citation accidents
- **Air Safety Institute** — AOPA's safety analysis video series; Early Analysis videos on notable accidents
- **Aviation News Talk Podcast** — Episode 385: N666DS Citation S550 San Diego crash analysis (pilot fatigue, approach below minimums)

## Maintenance & Technical Networks

### Textron Aviation (Cessna) Customer Support
- **Phone**: 1-800-4CESSNA (1-800-423-7762)
- **Website**: [txtav.com/support](https://txtav.com)
- **Parts**: Cessna / Textron Aviation parts ordering
- **Technical Support**: Factory technical representatives

### SkyWay MRO
- Specializes in Citation S550; well-regarded by COPA community for pre-purchase inspections and phase maintenance

### Duncan Aviation
- **Website**: [duncanaviation.aero](https://duncanaviation.aero)
- **Description**: Major Citation MRO with extensive Citation expertise
- **Knowledge Base**: Free technical articles and whitepapers

### West Star Aviation
- **Website**: [weststaraviation.com](https://weststaraviation.com)
- **Description**: Citation-authorized maintenance and avionics

### JETTECH
- **Location**: Centennial, CO
- **Specialty**: Garmin avionics STCs for S550 (G600/G700 TXi, GTN 650/750)
- **Reputation**: Well-known STC holder in the COPA community for Citation glass upgrades

## Aviation Publications

| Publication | Focus | Website |
|---|---|---|
| Business & Commercial Aviation | Business aviation operations | aviationweek.com/bca |
| AOPA Pilot | General aviation — some business jet content | aopa.org |
| Aviation Safety | Safety-focused analysis | aviationsafetymagazine.com |
| Professional Pilot | Professional pilot operations | propilotmag.com |
| AIN (Aviation International News) | Business aviation news | ainonline.com |

## Events & Conventions

- **NBAA-BACE** (Annual, October) — Las Vegas, NV — premier business aviation event
- **COPA Annual Convention** — Citation-focused seminars, safety, networking
- **Sun 'n Fun** — General aviation/business aviation (Lakeland, FL)
- **OSH (EAA AirVenture)** — Oshkosh, WI — broad aviation community

---

*Community resources are provided for reference. Always verify accuracy of technical information through official documentation and qualified mechanics.*

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.content:
# Frequently Asked Questions — Citation S550

## General

**Q: What is the difference between the Citation II (550) and the Citation S/II (S550)?**

A: The primary difference is the engine. The standard Citation II uses Pratt & Whitney Canada **JT15D-4** engines producing 2,300 lbf each. The S550 uses uprated **JT15D-4B** engines producing 2,500 lbf each — a 200 lb thrust increase per engine. This improves hot-and-high performance, single-engine climb rate, and overall margins. The S550 also features aerodynamic improvements including revised nacelle fairings and wing root treatment. Both share the same type certificate (CE-500 type rating).

---

**Q: Does the S550 require a type rating?**

A: Yes. The Citation S550 requires a **CE-500 type rating** for pilot-in-command privileges. This applies to all aircraft in the CE-500 type certificate group (Citation I/II/S/II family). Single-pilot operations require an additional single-pilot authorization (FAA exemption — see Training section). Training is available through FlightSafety, CAE, SIMCOM, and Textron Aviation training.

---

**Q: Can the S550 be flown single-pilot?**

A: Yes, but only under an **FAA Single-Pilot Exemption** — not a standard single-pilot designation. The S550 was certified as a Part 25 aircraft requiring two crew. Single-pilot ops require: (1) CE-500 type rating with single-pilot practical test, (2) minimum 500 turbine hours, (3) annual recurrent training, and (4) the exemption is valid for Part 91 US operations only — NOT internationally without foreign CAA authorization.

---

**Q: What is the approximate range of the S550?**

A: With NBAA IFR reserves, the S550 has a range of approximately **1,460 nautical miles** at long-range cruise. At higher cruise power settings, range is reduced. Maximum fuel (770 US gallons / ~5,163 lbs) provides approximately 3.5–4.5 hours of flight time depending on conditions and routing.

---

## Operations

**Q: Is RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum) required for the S550?**

A: Aircraft operating in RVSM airspace (FL290–FL410 in the US) must be RVSM-approved. Most S550s flying today have been RVSM approved through a modification/inspection program. Operators should verify their specific aircraft's RVSM approval status through their operations specifications or Letter of Authorization (LOA). Non-RVSM aircraft are restricted to FL280 and below (or FL430+ where permitted).

---

**Q: What fuel does the S550 use?**

A: The S550 is approved for **Jet-A and Jet-A1** as primary fuel types. JP-8 (military equivalent) is also approved. Jet-B (JP-4) may be used with limitations — consult the AFM for specific approved fuels and additives. The aircraft uses a standard single-point pressure refueling system as well as overwing fill points.

---

**Q: What airports can the S550 access?**

A: The S550 is a relatively short-field capable light jet. With a typical takeoff distance of ~3,250 ft at sea level (MTOW) and landing distance of ~2,700 ft, it can access many regional airports. Performance degrades at high elevation and high temperature airports — always compute actual performance using the AFM for the specific conditions. A **runway length of 4,000–5,000 ft** is a common practical minimum for comfortable operations.

---

**Q: What is the real-world fuel burn?**

A: Published specs show ~165–193 GPH. Owner community data from COPA and forums reports approximately **1,000 lb/hr at FL330** and **850 lb/hr at FL350**. At current Jet-A prices (~$6/gal), budget approximately **$1,000–$1,150/hr** in fuel costs at typical cruise altitudes.

---

## Maintenance & Ownership

**Q: What is the typical annual maintenance cost for an S550?**

A: Annual maintenance costs for a well-maintained S550 typically run **$80,000–$200,000** depending on utilization and what major events fall in a given year. Engine hot sections ($80,000–$150,000 per engine) and major phase inspections are the largest periodic costs. Phase V can run $75,000–$200,000. Budget conservatively — unplanned repairs on aging aircraft can be significant. Engine overhaul at TBO can exceed $1M for both engines.

---

**Q: What should I look for in a pre-purchase inspection?**

A: Key areas for a PPI include:
- Complete logbook review (airframe, both engines)
- AD compliance status verification (no open ADs; verify AD 2006-06-07 fuel boost pump compliance)
- Service bulletin review and compliance status
- Engine borescope inspection and trend data review
- **Corrosion inspection** — specifically under lavatory, beneath entry door, around belly antennas, wing leading edges (especially TKS aircraft)
- **Windshield condition** — cracking at attachment screws is a known recurring issue
- Avionics functionality and ADS-B Out compliance
- Pressurization and environmental system check
- Landing gear actuator condition
- Full avionics and autopilot functional check
- Fuel system integrity
- **Fire extinguisher wiring** — verify correct terminal connections

Always use a **qualified Citation service center** (SkyWay MRO recommended) for the PPI — not a general A&P. Budget **$5,000–$15,000** for a thorough pre-purchase inspection.

---

**Q: What is ADS-B Out and is the S550 required to have it?**

A: ADS-B Out (Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast) transmits aircraft position to ATC and other equipped aircraft. It became **FAA mandatory on January 1, 2020** for operations in most controlled airspace above 10,000 ft MSL and Class B/C surface areas. Aircraft operating without ADS-B Out in these areas face significant restrictions. Non-compliant aircraft are heavily discounted on the used market.

---

**Q: Where can I find Citation S550 parts?**

A: Key parts sources include:
- **Textron Aviation (Cessna) Parts**: Factory-new parts via authorized distributors
- **Aircraft Spruce & Specialty**: General aviation parts including certified components
- **Trade-A-Plane / Controller.com**: Used/overhauled parts marketplace
- **Western Aircraft, Duncan Aviation**: Citation-specific parts and overhaul
- **ILS (Inventory Locator Service)**: aviationpartssearch.com — parts database
- **AVCO, Aviall (Boeing)**: Authorized aviation parts distributors

---

*Answers provided are general reference information. For specific operational, maintenance, or regulatory questions, consult the official AFM, an FAA-certificated mechanic/IA, or an aviation attorney as appropriate.*

accidents.title: Accidents & Incidents
accidents.content:
# Accidents & Incidents — Citation S550 / Citation 550 Family

> **Purpose:** This section documents notable accidents and incidents involving the Citation S550 and closely related Citation 550 family aircraft. The goal is safety education — understanding what has gone wrong helps owners, operators, and pilots avoid similar outcomes. Always consult official NTSB reports for authoritative findings.

> **Note:** Not all accidents listed involve the S550 specifically — some involve the closely related Citation 550 (Citation II). Both share the CE-500 type certificate. Lessons apply across the family.

## Recent Major Accidents

---

### N666DS — San Diego, California (May 22, 2025)

**Aircraft:** Cessna Citation S550 (S/II)
**Registration:** N666DS
**NTSB Case:** WPR25FA161
**Fatalities:** 6 (all aboard)

**What happened:** At 3:47 AM PDT, the aircraft struck high-voltage power lines while on approach to Montgomery-Gibbs Executive Airport (KMYF) in thick fog. This was the second leg of an overnight flight originating from Teterboro, NJ, with a fuel stop at Wichita Jabara Field.

**Key factors under investigation:**
- **Pilot fatigue**: Pilot had been flying over 7.5 hours; crash occurred at 3:45 AM Pacific (6:45 AM Eastern), deep in the circadian low period
- **Inoperative ASOS**: The weather reporting equipment at KMYF was inoperative — the crew had no local altimeter setting
- **Approach execution**: Flight data suggests the pilot may have flown the LPV approach as if it were a non-precision LNAV approach, observing the PALOS fix step-down which applies only to LNAV. The aircraft was **173 feet below LPV minimums** and 440 feet below LNAV minimums when it leveled at 500 feet

**Safety lessons:**
- Fatigue management on overnight/multi-leg flights is critical
- LPV and LNAV approaches have different minima — know which you're flying
- Inoperative ASOS/AWOS at destination should trigger serious go/no-go reconsideration
- Brief approaches carefully after long flights when cognitive performance is degraded

**References:**
- [NTSB WPR25FA161 preliminary report](https://data.ntsb.gov/carol-repgen/api/Aviation/ReportMain/GenerateNewestReport/200192/pdf)
- [AOPA Air Safety Institute Early Analysis](https://www.aopa.org/news-and-media/all-news/2025/may/23/pr-aopas-air-safety-institute-releases-early-analysis-on-cessna-citation-crash-in-san-diego)
- [Aviation News Talk Podcast Episode 385](https://aviationnewstalk.com/podcast/385-n666ds-citation-s550-crash-in-san-diego-flying-below-minimums/) — detailed analysis
- [Katzman Lampert & Stoll analysis](https://katzmanlampert.com/cessna-550-crash-in-san-diego-pilot-fatigue-and-inoperative-asos-cited-as-factors/)

---

### N257BW — Statesville, North Carolina (December 18, 2025)

**Aircraft:** Cessna Citation 550 (Citation II), manufactured 1981
**Registration:** N257BW
**Owner:** GB Aviation Leasing LLC (Greg Biffle)
**Fatalities:** 7 (all aboard) — including former NASCAR driver Greg Biffle, his wife, and two children

**What happened:** The aircraft departed Statesville Regional Airport (SVNC) at 10:06 AM on runway 10. After traveling approximately 11 miles, the aircraft turned sharply left and returned toward the airport. During the return, the crew reported "problems" on radio. Recorded aircraft data showed gaps in airspeed and heading information and the autopilot disengaged. The aircraft descended and struck a 29 ft approach light structure about 1,380 ft from the runway, then additional light structures and trees before coming to rest 400 ft from runway end.

**Key NTSB preliminary findings:**
- **Pilot qualification issues**: Left seat captain's CE-500 type rating required a qualified SIC. Neither the right-seat pilot (Jack Dutton) nor Greg Biffle held the required endorsement to serve as qualified SIC
- **Electrical/avionics anomaly**: Cockpit communications referenced an "alternator" problem (the 550 has no alternator — it has starter-generators); Garmin system reportedly resumed functioning after crew action
- **Three persons in cockpit**: Investigation revealed three pilots were aboard, adding complexity to understanding the crew resource management
- **CVR recovered**: Cockpit Voice Recorder was recovered and is part of the NTSB investigation

**Safety lessons:**
- SIC qualification requirements are legal requirements — not suggestions. Verify endorsements before flight
- Understanding your aircraft's electrical system is fundamental — the 550 has starter-generators, not alternators
- Partial electrical failures require immediate, trained response
- CRM complexity increases with non-standard cockpit crew arrangements

**References:**
- [Wikipedia: 2025 North Carolina Cessna Citation II crash](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_North_Carolina_Cessna_Citation_II_crash)
- [NTSB Preliminary Report — AvWeb](https://avweb.com/flight-safety/accidents-ntsb/ntsb-greg-biffle-crash-preliminary-report/)
- [AIN coverage](https://www.ainonline.com/aviation-news/aerospace/2025-12-19/ex-nascar-driver-biffle-among-seven-killed-nc-crash)

---

### N550BP — Milwaukee, Wisconsin / Lake Michigan (June 4, 2007)

**Aircraft:** Cessna 550 Citation II
**Registration:** N550BP (Marlin Air)
**NTSB Report:** NTSB/AAR-09/06
**Fatalities:** 2

**What happened:** Aircraft impacted Lake Michigan shortly after departure from Milwaukee. The NTSB issued a full accident report (AAR-09/06) on this accident.

**References:**
- [NTSB AAR-09/06](https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/AccidentReports/Reports/AAR0906.pdf)

---

## Systemic Safety Themes (Citation 550 Family)

Reviewing the accident history of the Citation 550 family, several recurring themes emerge:

| Theme | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Approach below minimums / terrain clearance** | N666DS (San Diego 2025) — 173 ft below LPV minimums |
| **Pilot qualification deficiencies** | N257BW (Statesville 2025) — unqualified SIC |
| **Fatigue / human factors** | N666DS — 7.5 hrs flying, 3:45 AM local crash time |
| **Electrical/systems anomaly** | N257BW — avionics gaps, autopilot disconnect |
| **Inoperative ground equipment** | N666DS — ASOS inoperative, no local altimeter |

## Safety Resources

- **NTSB Aviation Accident Database**: [ntsb.gov/Pages/AviationQueryv2.aspx](https://www.ntsb.gov/Pages/AviationQueryv2.aspx) — search by aircraft type C550 or S550
- **Aviation Safety Network**: [aviation-safety.net/asndb/type/C550](https://aviation-safety.net/asndb/type/C550) — S550/550 accident database
- **AOPA Air Safety Institute Early Analysis**: [aopa.org/training-and-safety/online-learning/early-analysis](https://www.aopa.org/training-and-safety/online-learning/early-analysis)
- **Blancolirio (Juan Browne)** — YouTube channel with accident analysis videos
- **COPA Safety Resources** — [copa.org](https://copa.org) — Citation-specific safety data and discussions

---

*This section is for safety education only. Accident investigations are complex; preliminary reports may be revised. Always consult official final NTSB reports for authoritative determinations.*

prebuy.title: Pre-Purchase Guide
prebuy.content:
# Pre-Purchase Guide — Citation S550

Buying a Citation S550 is a significant investment. A thorough pre-purchase inspection (PPI) is not optional — it is the single best tool you have to avoid buying someone else's problem. This section provides a practical framework based on owner community experience.

## Budget for the PPI

**PPI cost: $5,000–$15,000** depending on MRO, aircraft location, and scope.

This is money well spent. A PPI may reveal $100,000+ in deferred maintenance or hidden damage. Never skip it, never let the seller's shop do it, and never let the seller attend.

## Choose the Right MRO

**Use an independent shop that specializes in Citations — not the broker's recommended shop, not a general A&P.**

- **SkyWay MRO** — frequently recommended by COPA community; S550-specific expertise
- **Textron Aviation Service Centers** — factory-authorized; good for definitive findings
- **Duncan Aviation** — capable, multi-location, Citation experience

> The MRO must be truly independent — no relationship with the seller or broker. Ask directly.

## Market Value Reference

- **Average 2025 market price**: ~$840,000 (PAPI data)
- **Well-equipped / glass cockpit**: $1,000,000–$1,500,000+
- **Needs work / older avionics**: $500,000–$750,000
- **Project aircraft**: Below $500,000 — buyer beware

Use **VREF** or **Aircraft Bluebook** for current valuations. Broker asking prices are not market prices.

## Pre-Purchase Inspection Checklist

### Logbook & Records Review (Before Physical Inspection)

- [ ] Airframe logbook — verify hours, check for damage history, major repairs
- [ ] Engine logbooks (both engines) — time since new, HSI, overhaul dates
- [ ] Prop/fan blade records
- [ ] AD compliance log — verify all ADs are closed, no open ADs
- [ ] Service bulletin compliance list
- [ ] STC list and data packages
- [ ] 337 field approvals on file
- [ ] Weight and balance current
- [ ] RVSM authorization (if flying above FL280)
- [ ] ADS-B Out installation 337 and compliance

### Physical Inspection — Airframe

#### Corrosion Hotspots (Inspect Carefully)
- [ ] **Under the lavatory** — remove panels, look for moisture damage
- [ ] **Beneath the entry door** — drain area, door sill, lower structure
- [ ] **Around all belly antennas** — sealant failures, water ingress
- [ ] **Wing leading edges** — especially if TKS-equipped (fluid residue traps moisture)
- [ ] Wing carry-through and spar cap areas
- [ ] Lower fuselage skin (belly area)
- [ ] Control surface hinges

#### Windshield
- [ ] Inspect for cracks — especially at attachment screws (known recurring issue)
- [ ] Check heat elements functional
- [ ] Replacement cost is significant — factor into offer if cracks present

#### Flight Controls
- [ ] Control column bobweight supports — cracks per Transport Canada Alert AL 1999-05
- [ ] Elevator hinge condition
- [ ] Aileron and rudder hinge play
- [ ] Trim tab condition

#### Landing Gear
- [ ] Actuator condition and time remaining
- [ ] Shimmy dampener condition
- [ ] Tire and brake wear
- [ ] Gear door condition and rigging

### Physical Inspection — Powerplant

- [ ] Borescope both engines (mandatory — do not skip)
- [ ] Engine trend monitoring data review — ITT, N1/N2, fuel flow trends
- [ ] Oil consumption records
- [ ] Engine mount condition
- [ ] Nacelle fairings and cowling condition
- [ ] Time since HSI (Hot Section Inspection) — 1,750 hr interval
- [ ] Time to TBO — 3,500 hrs (factor overhaul/extension STC cost into offer)
- [ ] TBO Extension STC status (if applicable)

### Physical Inspection — Systems

#### Fuel System
- [ ] Verify AD 2006-06-07 compliance (wing fuel boost pump wiring)
- [ ] Fuel tank visual inspection
- [ ] Crossfeed valve operation
- [ ] Boost pump operation test

#### Electrical
- [ ] Fire extinguisher bottle wiring — verify correct terminal connections (known mis-wiring issue)
- [ ] Battery load test
- [ ] Generator output check
- [ ] Bus tie operation
- [ ] All circuit breakers functional

#### Avionics
- [ ] All navigation radios functional (VOR, ILS, GPS)
- [ ] ADS-B Out operational and tested
- [ ] Autopilot — couple and fly all modes
- [ ] Weather radar functional
- [ ] Transponder and Mode C verified
- [ ] Garmin upgrades — verify STC documentation (JETTECH STCs for G600/G700/GTN)

#### Pressurization
- [ ] Outflow valve operation
- [ ] Safety valve operation
- [ ] Cabin climb rate test
- [ ] Pressurization controller calibration

### Test Flight

A test flight by a qualified Citation pilot is strongly recommended:
- Normal takeoff and climb
- High-altitude cruise check (ITT, N1/N2, fuel flow)
- Pressurization performance
- Autopilot coupling (ILS and GPS approaches)
- Single-engine demonstration (at altitude)
- Landing performance

## Engine Time — The Key Cost Driver

Engine time remaining is the single most important value/cost variable:

| Engines At | Budget For |
|---|---|
| Fresh HSI (<100 hrs since HSI) | Best position — $0 immediate engine cost |
| Mid-time (750–1,500 hrs since HSI) | HSI coming — reserve $80K–$150K per engine |
| Near TBO (>3,000 hrs since new or overhaul) | Full overhaul or TBO Extension STC — budget $500K–$1M+ |

> Always request an independent engine borescope AND trend data review. ITT creep is a red flag. Never rely on hours alone.

## Red Flags — Walk Away Conditions

- Open, uncorrected ADs (especially structural)
- Missing logbooks or logbook gaps
- Undisclosed damage history (check FAA records independently)
- Seller-controlled inspection or seller present during PPI
- Corrosion found in multiple locations
- ITT trending high or abnormal EGT spread between engines
- Non-compliant ADS-B (marketability issue and legal restriction)
- Avionics upgrades without proper STC documentation

## Negotiating After PPI

A PPI almost always finds something. Common negotiation points:
- Deferred phase inspection items
- Engine time to next HSI or TBO
- Avionics upgrade costs (glass vs. steam gauges)
- Windshield replacement
- Corrosion remediation

> Do not let the seller rush you or pressure you to close without adequate PPI time. A reputable seller expects a thorough inspection.

## Useful References

- **COPA Pre-Purchase Resources**: [copa.org](https://copa.org) — member forums have extensive pre-purchase threads
- **FAA Aircraft Registry**: [registry.faa.gov](https://registry.faa.gov) — verify ownership and registration
- **FAA AD Database**: [rgl.faa.gov](https://rgl.faa.gov) — verify open ADs
- **VREF / Aircraft Bluebook**: Current market valuations (subscription)
- **Controller.com / Trade-A-Plane**: Current market listings for comparable aircraft

---

*This guide is based on owner community experience and is provided for educational purposes. Always engage a qualified aviation attorney, A&P/IA mechanic, and financial advisor before any aircraft purchase.*
